text:
Department of Neurology

Clinic Date: 08 October 2019

Dr Amelia Grant
St Cuthbert’s Neurology Centre
Neurology Department, 14 Rosegate, Leeds LS2 8FA

Dear Dr Amelia Grant,

Alex Turner, DOB: 23-02-1996, Hospital No: NHS8473621 NHS No. 9081773342
Flat 3, 12 Riverbank Court, Leeds, LS9 7BT

Diagnoses:

Combined generalised and focal epilepsy: patient has both generalised and focal epilepsy. Prior history of generalised tonic-clonic seizures in adolescence; current predominant focal impaired-awareness seizures with occasional generalised spike–wave on EEG.

Current Medication:

- Levetiracetam 1,000 mg bd
- Lamotrigine 100 mg bd

Plan:

- Optimise treatment during current high-stress period; increase Lamotrigine to 125 mg bd and maintain Levetiracetam 1,000 mg bd; monitor tolerability and serum levels if clinically indicated.
- Rescue plan discussed for clusters; patient to use prescribed buccal midazolam as per safety plan and seek urgent care if clusters escalate.
- Lifestyle and trigger management reinforced; temporary academic adjustments advised given deterioration during exam/professional assessment season.
- Arrange urgent EEG with hyperventilation and sleep deprivation; book MRI brain (epilepsy protocol) if not updated within 3 years.
- Follow-up in 6 weeks, sooner if further deterioration.

I reviewed Alex Turner in clinic today. They report a clear deterioration coinciding with the current exam and professional assessment season, with heightened stress. Specifically, there have been four clusters this month; each ~four focal impaired-awareness seizures, typically beginning with a rising epigastric aura and behavioural arrest, progressing to oral automatisms and impaired awareness for 1–2 minutes, followed by confusion for up to 15 minutes. On two occasions, brief generalised myoclonic jerks were noted on awakening after poor sleep. There have been no recent generalised tonic–clonic seizures, but the pattern suggests both focal and generalised network involvement consistent with combined generalised and focal epilepsy.

Sleep has been curtailed to 5–6 hours on study days; caffeine intake is increased; stress levels are high. No missed doses were admitted, though timing has occasionally been delayed. No new intercurrent illness or alcohol use. Neurological examination today is non-focal. Review of prior EEG showed intermittent generalised spike–wave discharges and independent temporal sharp waves; last MRI (more than 3 years ago) was reported normal.

Given the recent escalation during a predictable trigger period (stress and sleep deprivation), I have recommended the above medication optimisation and reinforced use of a rescue plan for clusters. A fit note and a letter to the academic/professional body supporting reasonable adjustments have been provided. Safety advice regarding bathing, heights, and machinery was reiterated. Driving advice has been reviewed in line with DVLA guidance for seizures with impaired awareness.

Please do not hesitate to contact me if there are further concerns or if the frequency increases despite these adjustments.

With best wishes,

entities.text: there have been four clusters this month; each ~four focal impaired-awareness seizures
four clusters this month; each ~four focal impaired-awareness seizures

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: "there have been four clusters this month; each ~four focal impaired-awareness seizures." This provides both the number of clusters in the current month (4 per month) and the number of seizures per cluster (~4). No other higher-frequency figure is provided. Therefore, the appropriate normalized format is a cluster frequency with seizures per cluster.
